summary: This page asks a simple question: how easy is it for members to reach primary care where they live? The federal Health Resources & Services Administration (HRSA) designates parts of the country that are short of primary-care providers and scores each shortage area from 0 to 25 by severity.

We overlay the modeled member geography on those designations: how many members live in a shortage county, and how severe the shortage is where they live. The shortage designations are federal fact; which members live in them comes from our commute-based model.

moves.headline: Score every county
moves.detail: HRSA geographic primary-care HPSA designations, reduced to the worst designated score per county (designations can be sub-county; taking the worst is the standard conservative mapping).

citations.label: HRSA HPSA — Primary Care (designated, full detail)

moves.headline: Overlay the members
moves.detail: exposure = modeled members living in designated counties ÷ all members; severity = the member-weighted mean HPSA score. Member locations come from the commute-based member geography (see the Synthetic member profile methodology).

limitations: Shortage areas can be smaller than a county; mapping to counties is a standard approximation and can overstate exposure at the edges.
Which members live where is modeled from commute data, not actual addresses.
Primary care only in this version — dental and mental-health shortage designations are a straightforward extension.
